content: Protein consumption at night could decrease neurotransmitter production, potentially making it harder to fall asleep.
Acetylcholine levels are low during sleep, and protein-derived neurotransmitters are shut down in deep sleep stages.
Hypocretin levels, regulated by orxidipine, are increased by protein and decreased by carbohydrates, suggesting a higher carb, lower protein meal before bed may improve sleep.
For muscle building, a high-protein meal every 4-5 hours is recommended, including the last meal of the day.
If not focused on muscle building, a lower protein, higher carbohydrate meal before bed may help improve sleep quality.
